coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot decorated ultra-thin ZnO nanosheets for NO2 sensing at low temperatures
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yong-Hui
Wang, Chao-Nan
Yue, Li-Juan
Chen, Jun-Li
Gong, Fei-Long
Fang, Shao-Ming
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen dioxide (NO2), as a toxic gas, seriously harms the environment and human health. Semiconductor metal oxide (MOS) nanocomposites modified by N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have attracted extensive attention as sensing materials for NO2. Here, the N-GQDs modified ZnO composite material was successfully prepared by the hydrothermal method. Compared with pure ZnO, G-Z-2 (N-GQDS doping amount of 2 mL) exhibits excellent sensing performance for NO2. The G-Z-2 based sensor reduces the working temperature from 160 °C to 100 °C. The G-Z-2 is shown to be sensitive to 5 ppm NO2 and has a massively enhanced response of about 11.6 times. The detection limit was as low as 0.1 ppm. Moreover, it shows excellent reproducibility, selectivity and stability for the detection of NO2. The highly active N atom doping enhances the electron transfer of ZnO to N-GQDS and the adsorption of NO2 molecules. The heterojunction between ZnO and N-GQDS interface expands the resistance modulation, which improves the sensor sensitivity. This work can provide a promising strategy for improving the NO2 gas sensing performance based on semiconductor metal oxide.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO
N-GQDs
Heterojunction
Oxygen vacancy
Sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM of N-GQDs (b) XRD spectrum of N-GQDs (c) XRD spectrum of ZnO and N-GQDs/ZnO (d) Fourier infrared spectroscopy of ZnO, N-GQDs and G-Z-2.
(a–b) SEM of ZnO and G-Z-2 (c) EDS of G-Z-2 (d–g) TEM and HRTEM of ZnO and G-Z-2, respectively.
XPS spectrum of ZnO and G-Z-2, (a) C 1s (b) N 1s (c) O1s (d) Zn 2p.

(a) Response of ZnO and N-GQDs/ZnO composite at operating temperature of 80–240 °C. (b) Dynamic response curve of gas sensor to 0.1 ppm–100 ppm NO2. (c) Response curves of gas sensor to 5 ppm NO2. (d) Cycle reproducibility testing of G-Z-2 to 5 ppm NO2.
(a) Response of ZnO and G-Z-2 to 5 ppm different gas at 100 °C. (b) Long-time stability of sensors to 5 ppm NO2 at 100 °C.
A schematic diagram of gas sensing mechanism of pure ZnO and G-Z-2.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot decorated ultra-thin ZnO nanosheets for NO2 sensing at low temperatures

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Collaborative Innovation Center of Environmental Pollution Control and Ecological Restoration, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou, 450002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering
Collaborative Innovation Center of Environmental Pollution Control and Ecological Restoration

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Collaborative Innovation Center of Environmental Pollution Control and Ecological Restoration, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou, 450002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen dioxide (NO2), as a toxic gas, seriously harms the environment and human health. Semiconductor metal oxide (MOS) nanocomposites modified by N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have attracted extensive attention as sensing materials for NO2. Here, the N-GQDs modified ZnO composite material was successfully prepared by the hydrothermal method. Compared with pure ZnO, G-Z-2 (N-GQDS doping amount of 2 mL) exhibits excellent sensing performance for NO2. The G-Z-2 based sensor reduces the working temperature from 160 °C to 100 °C. The G-Z-2 is shown to be sensitive to 5 ppm NO2 and has a massively enhanced response of about 11.6 times. The detection limit was as low as 0.1 ppm. Moreover, it shows excellent reproducibility, selectivity and stability for the detection of NO2. The highly active N atom doping enhances the electron transfer of ZnO to N-GQDS and the adsorption of NO2 molecules. The heterojunction between ZnO and N-GQDS interface expands the resistance modulation, which improves the sensor sensitivity. This work can provide a promising strategy for improving the NO2 gas sensing performance based on semiconductor metal oxide.

ce:section.ce:para: NO2, a toxic gas with pungent odor, mainly comes from fossil fuel combustion and industrial production activities [1,2]. NO2 emission is the main cause of acid rain, photochemical smog and other pollution [3,4]. In addition, exposure to NO2 at the concentrations of 3 ppm could seriously harm human health, causing lung disease and respiratory infections, even death [5–7]. The NO2 produced by human metabolic activities could be used as a biomarker for medical diagnosis. It helps diagnose human diseases such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) [8]. Therefore, the realization of high sensitivity and selectivity detection of NO2 has an important meaning to environmental protection and physical safety.

ZnO, as a typical n-type semiconductor, is applied to photocatalysis, sensor, solar cells, piezoelectricity and other fields [9–12]. The gas sensors based on ZnO nanomaterials are used in the detection of toxic and harmful gases such as VOCs, NO2 and H2S [13–16]. However, low response and high operating temperature hinder the further application of ZnO as a sensing material [17–19]. The construction of heterojunction is one of the effective ways to improve ZnO gas sensitivity [20–22]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanoscale types of graphene with quantum constraints, edge effects, and adjustable electronic structures [23]. The gas sensing performance is significantly enhanced after complexing with metal oxides. Rahul Purbia et al. [24] prepared nitrogen-doped graphene dot/SnO2 quantum dot heterojunction using a simple wet chemical method. The sensing performance of low concentration NO2 was significantly enhanced. Ekasiddh Wongrat et al. [25] prepared a sensor based on ZnO nanostructures and composite graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which improved the sensitivity and selectivity to ammonia at room temperature. Chu et al. [26] prepared ZnFe2O4/graphene quantum dot nanocomposites by hydrothermal method, showing excellent gas sensitivity to acetone. The reasons for improving the gas sensitivity of GQDS-metal oxide composites could be attributed to the conjugated π structure of GQDS, the dangling bond defect on the edge of the graphene layer, high specific surface area and excellent electron mobility [27,28]. Further, doping of electronegative nitrogen atom greatly changes the charge transfer between GQDS and ZnO. It provides more active sites, and more suitable for resistive gas sensors. However, in the low concentration of NO2, reducing the working temperature and improving the response is still a serious challenge for sensors.

In this work, the N-GQDs/ZnO composites were successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method. The gas sensitivity test results show that the N-GQDs/ZnO exhibits an excellent sensing performance for NO2. Furthermore, G-Z-2 composites significantly reduced the working temperature to 100 °C. The response value of the sensor based on G-Z-2 to 5 ppm NO2 increased by 11.6 times, and the detection limit is as low as 0.1 ppm. The improvement of gas sensitivity is attributed to the synergistic effect of N-GQDs/ZnO heterojunction, highly reactive N atoms and oxygen vacancy.

The synthesis process of N-GQDs is shown in Fig. S1. Dissolve 1 mmol citric acid and 3 mmol urea in 5 mL deionized water. After stirring to clarify, the solution was transferred to a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene, and react at 160 °C for 4 h. The mixture was centrifuged at a low speed (5000 r/min), and the supernatant obtained was N-GQDs.

The method of synthesis of ultra-thin ZnO nanocrystals is similar to our previous reports [29]. In the step of ZnO synthesis, N-GQDs were dropped before the high temperature reaction. Then the mixed solution was reacted at 120 °C for 12 h. The obtained white precipitate was washed three times with absolute ethanol and deionized water. After drying at 60 °C for 12 h and calcining at 400 °C for 2 h, N-GQDs/ZnO composites were obtained. According to the different amount of N-GQDs solution (0 mL, 1 mL, 1.5 mL, 2 mL, 2.5 mL, 3 mL), the obtained N-GQDs/ZnO composites were named as: ZnO, G-Z-1, G-Z-1.5, G-Z-2, G-Z-2.5, G-Z-3.

The crystal structure and morphology of the materials were characterized by the X-ray diffraction (XRD, a Rigaku D/Max-2550 diffractometer, 50 kV), the emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-7001F, 10 kV), the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, 200 kV) and energy dispersion spectrum (EDS, S-4800, Hitachi). The ZnO and N-GQDs/ZnO were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a ESCALAB-250Xi spectrometer to further determine the combined state of elemental and the interaction between N-GQDs and ZnO. The sensor performance of the material was tested using the WS-30A test system.

The detailed production process of the gas sensing element is as follows. The prepared sample was ground in an agate mortar for 30 min, then terpineol was added and mix until uniform. Then the slurry was evenly coated on the surface of the alumina ceramic tube, dried at 80 °C for 30min, and calcined in air at 400 °C for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. Then, the nickel chromium heating wire is inserted into the ceramic tube, which was welded to the base of the ceramic tube. Before the gas sensing test, the prepared gas sensor was put on the aging table and aged at 300 °C for 10 days to enhance the stability of the device. The WS-30A gas sensor test system is used to test the gas sensitivity of the gas sensor. The details of the testing process are provided in supporting information. The response value of the sensor is defined as S (SRa/Rg reductive gas, S = Rg/Ra oxidizing gas), Ra is the resistance of the sensor in air, Rg is the resistance in the target gas [30,31]. The sensor response time represents the time required for the resistance to reach 90% of the equilibrium value after injection of the target gas. Recovery time refers to the time required for the resistance to recover to 10% of the equilibrium value after the measured gas removed [32–34].

The structure and morphology of N-GQDs were characterized by TEM, HRTEM and XRD. In Fig. 1 (a), the N-GQDs with a diameter of about 3–5 nm is uniformly dispersed. The lattice-plane spacing of 0.32 nm were corresponding to the N-GQDS (110) plane. Fig. 2 (b) shows the XRD spectrum of the N-GQDs. There is a wide diffraction peak at about 2θ = 23.2. According to Bragg's law: (2dsinθ = nλ, where n represents integers of a given order and λ represents wavelengths) the calculated spacing D value (0.37 nm) is consistent with the original graphene film spacing [35]. In Fig. 1(c), there are diffraction peaks were assigned to the (100), (002), (101), (102) and (110) crystal planes. The obtained lattice constants are a = b = 0.325 nm, c = 0.520 nm, indicating that ZnO is in the hexagonal wurtzite crystal state. There is no N-GQDs diffraction peak in the composite because of the low composite amount of N-GQDs and the weak diffraction peak intensity. After modification by N-GQDS, the diffraction peak of the composite is shifted to a high angle due to the interaction between N-GQDS and ZnO. As shown in Fig. 4(d) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDS, the wide absorption band of 3000–3500 cm−1 is according to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H [23]. N-GQDs exhibits strong hydrophilicity because of many amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface [28]. The bands of 1707 cm−1 and 1665 cm−1 are attributed to the absorption bands of vibrational carbon and oxygen in COOH and CONH, respectively [36]. The absorption bands at 1577 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1 are caused by CC and C–N bending vibrations [37].

The morphology and microstructure of ZnO and G-Z-2 were researched by FESEM, TEM and HRTEM. In Fig. 2(a–b), the pure ZnO expresses ultrathin nanosheet structure. However, the composite materials morphology changes from nanosheet to spherical after N-GQDs modification. It could be observed from Fig. 2(b) that the spherical structure is formed from nanosheets. In Fig. 2(c), the C, N, O and Zn elements are evenly distributed, which proves that N-GQDs were successfully deposited on ZnO materials. Fig. 2(d–g) shows the TEM and HRTEM images of ZnO and G-Z-2, respectively. The ultrathin ZnO nanosheets are mesoporous, and the lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.28 nm consisted with the (100) crystal plane of the wurtzite ZnO. The G-Z-2 maintained the porous structure. The lattice spaces with 0.28 nm and 0.21 nm can be assigned to ZnO (100) plane and N-GQDS (100) plane, respectively. It proves that the heterojunction was formed between ZnO and N-GQDs [28].

The surface composition and compound states of ZnO and G-Z-2 were detected by XPS. The C 1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 3 (a) shows that there is a main sp-carbon peak at 284.95 eV. The diffraction peaks at 286.65 eV is attributed to C–N and C–O, which are the products of sp2-hybridized carbon. The relative strength of COOH (288.5 eV) peak of G-Z-2 was increased. It is attributed to the overlap of COOH peak corresponding to N-GQDs near the same binding energy. In addition, the corresponding hexagonal pyridine (399.7 eV) and nitrogen atom diffraction peaks are shown in Fig. 3(b). In Fig. 3(c), the O 1s diffraction peak of G-Z-2 is fitted into three peaks with centers located at 530 eV, 531.4 eV and 532.9 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen (OL), oxygen vacancy (OV) and adsorbed oxygen (OC) [38,39]. The results of fitting analysis showed that the Ov content in G-Z-2 increased from 16.2% to 40.0%. In Fig. 3(d), the binding energies of 1021.0 eV, 1044.1 eV, 1020.6 eV and 1043.9 eV correspond to the characteristic peaks of Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of ZnO and G-Z-2, respectively [40,41]. In G-Z-2 composites, the electron is transferred from ZnO to N-GQDS, which leads the diffraction peak of Zn shifts to the high binding energy.

Fig. 4 shows the gas sensing performance diagram based on ultrathin ZnO nanosheets and N-GQDs/ZnO composite. Fig. 4 (a) shows the linear relationship between gas sensing response and working temperature in 5 ppm NO2 atmosphere. The result shows that the best working temperature of pure ZnO is 160 °C. After N-GQDs modification, the best working temperature reduces to 100 °C, and the sensitivity is obviously enhanced. The adsorption of NO2 and redox reactions require high temperature to overcome specific energy barriers, therefore pure ZnO shows good gas sensitivity at high temperature. After the highly electronegative N-atom doping, the basicity of N-GQDs/ZnO material increases and the binding energy of N-GQDS for NO2 adsorption decreases, resulting in the enhancement of NO2 adsorption on the material surface. Therefore, the G-Z-2-based sensor shows excellent sensitivity to NO2 at low temperatures. With the increase of temperature, the adsorption capacity of N-GQDs on NO2 decreases, which reduces the sensing performance at high temperature. Fig. 4 (b) shows the dynamic response of ZnO and G-Z-2 sensor to 0.1–100 ppm NO2 at 100 °C, indicating that the detection limit of G-Z-2 sensor is lower than 0.1 ppm. The theoretical LOD is presented according to the following equation:
(1)
LOD
( ppb )
= 3 σ / s
where σ and s are the standard deviation and the slope of the calibration curve, respectively. The 40 data points are collected before exposure to NO2, and σ was calculated to be 0.0140. The s is 0.942, which is obtained from the fitting curve shown in Fig. S2. The LOD is calculated as low as 44.5 ppb. Then, the sensing properties of ZnO and N-GQDs/ZnO composites to 5 ppm NO2 were further analyzed under the condition of 100 °C (Fig. 4(c)). The response of G-Z-2 (58) to NO2 was 11.6 times higher than that of ZnO (5). N-GQDs are uniformly dispersed on ZnO materials to form a more effective heterojunction interface. It improves the gas sensitivity by promoting the charge transport between the material interface. When N-GQDS doping is 2 mL, the material exhibits the best gas sensitivity to NO2. With the increase of N-GQDs doping amount, excessive N-GQDs agglomerate on the ZnO surface, which impedes the charge transport of interface heterojunction and reduces the sensitivity to NO2 [27,42]. As shown in Fig. 4(d), G-Z-2 showed excellent reproducibility after consecutive reproducibility tests. The gas sensitivity test of 5 ppm different gas at 100 °C shows that G-Z-2 exhibits selectivity to NO2 (Fig. 5 (a)). Fig. 5(b) shows a 30-day stability test on ZnO and G-Z-2, which shows that G-Z-2 has excellent stability. The gas sensitivity of G-Z-2 was tested in different humidity environment to study the moisture resistance of the sensor. The test results show that the response value is as high as 58 under 80% RH environment. As the RH reaches 20%, the response value decreases significantly. Therefore, water vapor has an adverse effect on the sensor response. In humid air, the water molecules compete for adsorption sites with NO2 gas and adsorb on the surface of sensing layer [20]. Therefore, the sensing performance decreases in the presence of water vapor (Fig. S3).

The sensing process of metal oxide semiconductor is the adsorption-desorption of oxygen molecules on the material surface [43]. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the O2 adsorbed on the surface of the material traps electrons from the ZnO conductive band to form active oxygen ions. An electron depletion layer is formed on the surface of ZnO, and the resistance increases [44,45]. When the detection system is transferred to NO2 atmosphere, NO2 molecules capture electrons of the material to form adsorbed NO2 −, or react with O− to form NO3 − (Fig. 6(b)). This results in a thicker electron depletion layer and a higher barrier, which increases the resistance of the sensor [43,46,47]. The reaction process expresses in the following equation:
(2)
N
O 2
(
g a s
)
+
e −
→ N
O 2
−
( a d s )
(3)
N
O 2
(
g a s
)
+
O −
→ N
O 3
−
( a d s )

The reasons for the enhancement of NO2 sensitivity by G-Z-2 composites are as follows: First, as a strong electron-sucking molecule, NO2 is easily adsorbed on the electron rich sites [28]. When N atoms are introduced into GQDs, NO2 molecules preferentially bind to N atoms due to the basicity of N-containing groups [36]. The adsorption of NO2 on G-Z-2 surface is enhanced, and the charge transfer between NO2 molecule and N-GQDS surface is increased. The redox reaction between ZnO and the ionic oxygen promotes the precipitation of electrons, and increases the resistance. In addition, The doping of N atoms leads to the formation of p-n heterojunction in N-GQDS. The p-n heterojunction effectively promotes the carrier separation and transfer [48]. Secondly, the heterojunction formed between ZnO and G-Z-2 interface plays a leading role in improving gas sensitivity response. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the work function of ZnO (4.45 eV) is lower than that of N-GQDs (5.2 eV). When N-GQDs in G-Z-2 contact with ZnO, the electrons flow from ZnO to N-GQDs until Fermi level reaches equilibrium. The band bending near the ZnO and N-GQDs interface forms an electron depletion layer. The heterojunction promotes the rapid separation of carriers and reduces the interfacial charge transfer resistance [49,50]. When G-Z-2 transferred to NO2 atmosphere, the adsorption of NO2 molecules is enhanced, and more electrons are captured from G-Z-2 composites. It results in a thicker electron depletion layer at the heterogeneous interface [51]. Therefore, the sensor resistance regulation is expanded and the sensitivity to NO2 is enhanced. Thirdly, after the introduction of N-GQDS, the content of oxygen vacancy in the material increased significantly. It is beneficial to provide more active sites, increase the concentration of free charge in the material, and promote the chemical reaction on the surface of the material [52,53].

In summary, N-GQDS modified ZnO composite was successfully prepared by hydrothermal method. The gas sensitive test results show that the sensor based on G-Z-2 effectively reduces the working temperature. The response to NO2 was significantly enhanced and the detection limit was as low as 0.1 ppm. Furthermore, it has excellent selectivity and stability. The excellent sensing performance of G-Z-2 is mainly due to the positive effects of heterojunction, active N atom doping and oxygen vacancy. Overall, our results offered an effective strategy with the advantage of high response and low detection limit to reduce the operating temperature to the detection of NO2.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to National Natural Science Foundation of China (21771166, U1704256, U2004196), Center Plain Science and Technology Innovation Talents (194200510013).
